coredata.dc:title: Degradation evolution of tetracycline-HCl/Fluoroquinolones and CO2 reduction over novel Z-scheme NCQDs-black NiO/Bi7O9I3 mesopores nanosheet heterojunction

coredata.dc:creator: Mohammadi, Ali
Haghighi, Mohammad
Shabani, Maryam
coredata.dc:description: The novel mesopores nanosheet Z-scheme NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 (NCQDs: nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots & BNiO: black nickel oxide) heterojunction nanophotocatalyst has been synthesized by the facile sono-solvothermal method in order to remove antibiotic/fluoroquinolones and solid/liquid phase photocatalytic reduction of CO2 under the irradiation of simulated solar light. Several analyzes, have been performed to investigate the physical, chemical and optical properties of the synthesized photocatalysts. Raman analysis of NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 sample shows the increase of structural defects which is the result of the presence of NCQDs in the structure of this sample. The characteristics of the pore volume, surface area and pore diameter of the optimal photocatalyst have been estimated at about 0.209 cm3/g, 44.6 m2/g and 18.8 nm, respectively. The UV–vis DRS analysis indicated the increase in optical absorption range and two absorption edges (373.7 and 526.4 nm). Pharmaceutical pollutants like tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH), ofloxacin, levofloxacin, and ciprofloxacin were removed by the photocatalytic degradation process in the order of 81.6%, 73.6%, 73.4% and 71.5%, respectively by NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 photocatalyst which it can be asserted because of efficacious happening of surface reactions, facilitation of mass transfer and surface adsorption, which shows the key effect of the created structural model and the usage of ultrasound waves. At the end the photoreduction activity of the quintessential photocatalyst was evaluated which could produce 43.437 µmol/gcatalyst methanol after 8 h.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The novel mesopores nanosheet Z-scheme NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 (NCQDs: nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots & BNiO: black nickel oxide) heterojunction nanophotocatalyst has been synthesized by the facile sono-solvothermal method in order to remove antibiotic/fluoroquinolones and solid/liquid phase photocatalytic reduction of CO2 under the irradiation of simulated solar light. Several analyzes, have been performed to investigate the physical, chemical and optical properties of the synthesized photocatalysts. Raman analysis of NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 sample shows the increase of structural defects which is the result of the presence of NCQDs in the structure of this sample. The characteristics of the pore volume, surface area and pore diameter of the optimal photocatalyst have been estimated at about 0.209 cm3/g, 44.6 m2/g and 18.8 nm, respectively. The UV–vis DRS analysis indicated the increase in optical absorption range and two absorption edges (373.7 and 526.4 nm). Pharmaceutical pollutants like tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH), ofloxacin, levofloxacin, and ciprofloxacin were removed by the photocatalytic degradation process in the order of 81.6%, 73.6%, 73.4% and 71.5%, respectively by NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 photocatalyst which it can be asserted because of efficacious happening of surface reactions, facilitation of mass transfer and surface adsorption, which shows the key effect of the created structural model and the usage of ultrasound waves. At the end the photoreduction activity of the quintessential photocatalyst was evaluated which could produce 43.437 µmol/gcatalyst methanol after 8 h.

ce:section.ce:para: The birth of fluoroquinolones and antibiotics is one of the most valuable achievements of humans in the field of medicine and veterinary medicine. Tetracycline hydrochloride as the second most consumed antibiotic with a consumption rate of one third of the total production is often used in the field of agriculture and animal husbandry (Deylami et al., 2023; Khanmohammadi et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021). Approximately 50% of antibiotics are excreted from the body in the form of un-metabolized compounds such as urine due to incomplete absorption in the body of humans and animals, which causes contamination in the environment due to the stability and hydrophobicity of these substances that will eventually causes disturbances in the human food chain (Abdollahizadeh et al., 2021; Sar et al., 2022). Therefore, with the increasing population and the development of living standards of communities, it is paramount importance to solve the problems caused by these antibiotics (Sarafraz et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2013).

Over the past few years many studies and researches have been done to remove these antibiotics from the environment like advanced oxidation processes, adsorption/bio-sorption (Kariminejada et al., 2021), ozonation and biodegradation (Kakavandi et al., 2016; Kumar and Dutta, 2022; Ling et al., 2023). Among these methods, advanced oxidation processes (Amini et al., 2023; Mikaeeli et al., 2022; Mohseni et al., 2022) (AOPs) have garnered considerable attention which included several methods like: ozonation, photocatalytic (degradation & reduction) (Haghighi et al., 2017; Sokhansanj et al., 2023), Fenton or a combination of them as: photo-Fenton, UV/O3, UV/H2O2 and UV/TiO2 (Mirzai et al., 2020; Monteagudo et al., 2014). Among these methods, photocatalytic degradation because of its low cost (Chapari et al., 2022; Haghighi et al., 2019; Nadernia et al., 2022), removal of various types of contaminants especially toxic pollutants, environment friendly and non-toxicity is very useful to elimination of organic pollutants (Abdelraouf et al., 2022; Chang et al., 2010; Wu et al., 2022). In this process it uses photocatalysts which are very useful to oxidation of organic pollutants into CO2 and H2O.

Due to the daily industrialization of societies and desire for a comfortable life, the need for energy is increasing over days, and the most of this energy is provided by burning fossil fuels, which has destructive results such as the release of greenhouse gases such as carbon dioxide. One of the ways to reduce these greenhouse gases is to turn it into valuable products. One of the existing methods for this work, which is currently attracting the attention of most researchers, is the photocatalytic conversion of carbon dioxide gas (Collado et al., 2013; Mazari et al., 2021; Zhi et al., 2023).

Nickel oxide (NiO) has attracted a lot of attention due to its unique properties, such as having a large surface area as the base of photocatalysts; but due to its optical activity range (active under ultraviolet light radiation that consist of only 4% of sunlight radiation) and wide band gap energy (Aghamali et al., 2018), there is a need to develop photocatalysts active in the visible light region. Usage of carbon materials like carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanotubes (CNT) and carbon quantum dot doped with nitrogen (NCQDs) because of their stability, high aqueous solubility, non-toxicity, uniform distribution, excellent charge transport properties, easy functionalization and large surface area are solutions that can be opted to reduce the recombination rate of charge carriers and increase the light absorption region (Ke et al., 2017). Pebdeni et all found out that doping carbon quantum dots on nickel oxide could enhanced transferring of photogenerated charge carriers and increase photocatalyst activity (Pebdeni et al., 2021), in turn Nath et all prepared polystyrene-NiO-CNT to degradation of methylene blue dye (Nath and Mahato, 2022). Most Researchers have shown interest in composite photocatalysts (semiconductor-semiconductor heterojunction), like NiO Supported YMnO3 Nanoparticles which prepared by Weng et all (Wang and Song, 2020) and NiO/ZnO prepared by Gnanasekaran et all (Gnanasekaran et al., 2022), with this structure can effectively decrease the recombination rate and Increase the separation rate of photogenerated charge carriers. Bi-based semiconductors have been distinguished by researchers due to its cheap price and unique morphology but because of having narrow band gap energy (ranging from 2.20 eV to 2.24 eV) they have weaknesses as single-phase semiconductor like fast recombination rate of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, insufficient light absorption and relatively poor photocatalytic activity (Lin et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2018). It seems that the decoration of black nickel oxide by the usage of carbon materials such as carbon quantum dot doped with nitrogen can improve the optical properties, and create a heterojunction structure with bismuth-based semiconductors can also improve the band gap and reduce the recombination rate of charge carriers.

Herein, the novel mesopores nanosheet Z-scheme NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 (NCQDs: nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots & BNiO: black nickel oxide) heterojunction nanophotocatalyst has been synthesized by the facile sono-solvothermal method to liquid photoreduction of CO2 and remove tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) and fluoroquinolones under the simulated sun light illumination. The Black NiO, NCQDs-Black NiO, Black NiO/Bi7O9I3 and Bi7O9I3 were also synthesized and their properties and performance were compared with Z-scheme NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3. How the transportation of charge carriers in the created nano-heterostructure indicated the formation of an indirect structure of the Z-scheme. And regarding the perspective of band gap engineering, the charge separation highly improved, and the recombination rate of charge carriers was reduced. Besides, the role of holes, electrons, hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide radical (•O2 -) was determined in the removal of antibiotic/fluoroquinolones. And also, to further evaluate, the ability of Z-scheme NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 in the process of photocatalytic carbon dioxide reduction (PCR) was investigated.

Potassium iodide (KI), nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3).6 H2O), citric acid (C6H7O8) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) manufactured by Merck were used as required chemicals. Also, the required ethylene glycol (C2H6O2), ethanol (C2H5OH), and ammonia (NH3) and Bismuth Nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3.5 H2O) were obtained from Mojalli and Sigma-Aldrich Company, respectively. Industrial urea (used as agricultural fertilizer) was used as the required precursor. In addition, deionized water was also used in different stages of testing and synthesizing.

Synthesis of nanophotocatalyst was done in three steps which are clear in Fig. 1. first step demonstrates the synthesis of NCQDs-BNiO by sono-deposition methodology: in step (a) (Fig. 1(a)), BNiO and NCQDs that were previously synthesized dispersed in a mixture of ethanol and deionized water (volume ratio 2:1) by ultrasound waves with the power of 200 W for half an hour to achieve a well-dispersed solution. The obtained mixture was placed on a hot plate at a temperature of 50 °C; so that, while stirring, a large amount of solvents evaporated and the resulting mixture turn into a gel form. Finally, this gel was dried for 3 h at the temperature of 180 °C. Then after cooling to room temperature, the resulting black powder was mixed with deionized water several times and placed on a magnetic stirrer which the NCQDs that have not formed bonds with BNiO, dissolved in deionized water. Then the resulting mixture was filtered and the remaining precipitate was dried at room temperature. Finally, black nickel oxide decorated with nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots was obtained, which was named as NCQDs-BNiO.

In step (b) (Fig. 1(b)), NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 nanocomposite was synthesized by sono-solvothermal methodology. For this purpose, firstly, known amounts of potassium iodide were dissolved in 30 mL of ethylene glycol with a magnetic stirrer until a clear solution (A) was obtained. Meanwhile, the mixture of NCQDs-BNiO and solution containing ethanol and ethylene glycol (volume ratio: 3:4) was sonicated for half an hour (200 W) to achieve uniform solution. Next, the bismuth nitrate pentahydrate was employed at the stoichiometric amount and added to the previous suspension. Then, the mixture sonicated (200 W and 1 h) to gain an almost white suspension (B). Solution (A) was added by the form of dropwise to suspension (B) while mixing, and then the resulting slime green mixture was sonicated with a power of 200 W for 30 min. At the end, the final suspension was moved to a stainless-steel autoclave and placed in an oven with a temperature of 150°C for 18 h. After cooling the autoclave to room temperature, in the last step (post-treatment) (Fig. 1(c)), the obtained sediment is washed several times with deionized water and filtered through a sieve. Then, it was dried under the ambient air. Finally, NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 nanophotocatalyst was obtained. It is worth mentioning that NCQDs/Bi7O9I3, Bi7O9I3 and BNiO/Bi7O9I3 samples were synthesized in the same way as above, but with the difference that, in the synthesis of Bi7O9I3 nanophotocatalyst, NCQDs-BNiO sample was not added. Also, in the synthesis of NCQDs/Bi7O9I3, BNiO and in the synthesis of BNiO/Bi7O9I3, NCQDs were not added.

XRD analysis was carried out to identify the materials, evinced the correct synthesis of the demanded phases and their crystalline forms with a D-5000 powder diffractometer device manufactured by Siemens company (Germany), in the range of 2θ = 5◦ to 90◦ (for NCQDs) and 2θ = 10◦ to 80◦ for other concocted nanophotocatalysts. Raman spectroscopy, which is one of the most common techniques based on different molecular vibrations after absorbing light, was used to determine the presence of carbon compounds in the molecular structure of the synthesized nanomaterials and structural defects of them. FTIR analysis was done to investigate the functional groups of surfaces in the wave number for the range of 1000–4000 cm−1 for NCQDs and 400–4000 cm−1 for other samples. In order to investigate the surface structural of nanoparticles and study their morphology, as well as the approximate surface distribution of the particles in the structure of nanophotocatalysts, field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) analysis (by Mira 3-XMU device made by TESCAN Company in Czech Republic) has been applied. In order to investigate the surface morphology of nanoparticles as much as possible and to better identify the morphology structure of the synthesized material, an analysis called transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (by the Leo 906) has been used. To analyze and scan the 3D constitution of the materials, elevations on the surface of the prepared nanoparticles, determine the thickness of the plates, and determine the distribution and size of the particles on the nanoplates, Atomic force microscopy (AFM) analysis was applied. Brunauer–Emmett–Teller and the Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BET-BJH) analysis was used to determine and compare structural parameters like as porosity and pore volume (Vp), pore diameter, specific surface area (SBET), size distribution and kind of pore structure. The BET-BJH analysis which was obtained by the nitrogen adsorption/desorption mechanism (done by Chembet-3000 manufactured by Quanta Chrome, USA) was reported in this study. DRS analysis data (wavelength range between 260 and 710 nm) at room temperature according to the light absorbance of the nanomaterials is used to describe the optical properties of synthesized samples and determine their energy gap using a Scinco 4100 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The photoluminescence (PL) analysis is used as a suitable technique to evaluate the transport effects, separation and separation of charge carriers in nanophotocatalyst particles. PL shows the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs that are excited at a certain wavelength (excitation wavelength).

For evaluating the performance of the synthesized photocatalysts, reactor tests were carried out under the illumination of simulated sunlight by a 400 W halogen lamp without a UV cut-off filter (Which illuminates wavelengths higher than 315 nm) in order to remove fluoroquinolones as pharmaceutical contaminants especially tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) at room temperature. The molecular structure and physicochemical characteristics of the pollutants are mentioned in Table 1, so that, to check the ability of photocatalysts in degradation of these pollutants, the experiments were carried out as follows: each experiment was done in the same condition, so that 100 mL of tetracycline hydrochloride (70 mg/L) with 1 gr/L nanophotocatalyst was prepared. Subsequently, to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium between the photocatalyst surface and contaminant molecules, the mixture of nano photocatalyst and the contaminant solution was mixed with a magnetic stirrer in a dark condition under a box for 210 min. Afterwards, the test was continued for 180 min under the radiation of the chosen lamp which was introduced earlier. Additionally, an ice-water bath was used to prevent the temperature escalating. Sampling (4 mL of suspension) was done at the end of the adsorption/desorption stage and also at 30 min intervals until the end of the test (photo-degradation), then the nanophotocatalyst particles separated by centrifuge, then with applying of UV–vis-1800 spectrophotometer (UV-1800, BRAIC, China), concentration of remaining antibiotic in the solution at the maximum wavelength of 359 nm was detected.

Eq. (1) was used to calculate the pollutant degradation efficiency (percentage of pollutant removal), in which η, C0 and Ci represent the degradation efficiency, the initial concentration of the pollutant at the end of dark condition and the concentration of the pollutant at the time intervals (at each sampling), respectively.

To further survey the photocatalytic performance, the selected nanophotocatalyst was evaluated in the degradation of other antibiotic pollutants, such as ofloxacin, levofloxacin and ciprofloxacin. To clarify the active species participating in the process of photocatalytic degradation of pollutants, scavenger test was taken. The species that are often considered are: holes, electrons, hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide radical (•O2 -); Therefore, the effects of the following were investigated in the degradation process: ammonium oxalate (AO) as a cavity inhibitor, benzoquinone (BQ) as an •O2 - inhibitor and isopropyl alcohol as an •OH inhibitor.

To evaluate the photoreduction performance of the selected photocatalyst, a stainless steel with internal volume of 600 mL, which has a Pyrex glass on top of it, was used. Firstly 200 mL of deionized water with 0.2 g photocatalyst was introduced into the reactor, and then it was bubbled to purge the air and saturate the solution by fluid CO2 with a constant flow (50 mL/min) for 30 min while mixing with a magnetic stirrer. Then the inlet and outlet of the reactor was closed by the two on/off valves connected to them to be completely sealed and then the illumination (illuminates wavelengths higher than 315 nm) was started from the top of the reactor. Sampling (4 mL of suspension) was taken at 2 h intervals for 8 h. After separating the nanophotocatalyst particles by using a centrifuge, the concentration of methanol (MeOH) in the solution was detected with gas chromatograph (GC Chrom, Teif Gostar Faraz, Iran) equipped with a flame ionization detector (FID). The products were analyzed with HP-PLOTU and column. The sample was injected with a 10 µL syringe into the liner kept at 180 °C. Carrier gas: N2 (HP grade 99.999%), column temperature: start from 50 °C then increased with a rate of 2.5 °C/min to reach 120 °C for 5 min and detector temperature was 110 °C.

In Fig. 2(a), the result of X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD) of nitrogen-carbon quantum dot is presented. In this sample, a wide peak is observed at 2θ= 26.5˚, which refer to the [0 0 2] plane of the NCQDs. This broad peak indicates the graphite structure of this nanoparticle. Also, the larger interlayer spacing between lattice plates of NCQDs (d=0.335 nm) compared to graphite can be ascribed to the presence of nitrogen-containing functional groups that entered the structure of NCQDs through hydrothermal synthesis method.

The FTIR analysis to determine the functional groups of NCQDs in the wavenumber range of 1000–4000 cm−1 is presented in Fig. 2(b). The peaks at 1166, 1400, 1561 and 1680 cm−1 are related to stretching vibration bonds of C-NH-C/C-O, CC, -NH2 and CO respectively. Also, the broad vibration bond that appeared in the wavenumber range of 2800–3500 cm−1 indicates the three functional groups which are N-H, C-H and -OH. C-O absorption peaks at 1326 cm−1 and C-N at 1265 cm−1 wavenumber indicating the reaction between amino groups and carboxyl as a result of using urea for synthesis. As can be seen, there are various groups (such as carbonyl, carboxyl, hydroxyl, amine, and alkyl groups) on the surface of NCQDs, which causes the high solubility of this material in water and also increasing the electron accepting/donating property (Chandrasekaran et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2018).

In Fig. 2(c), Raman spectroscopy analysis also confirms the structural properties of NCQDs. As it can be seen, the Raman spectrum of the synthetic NCQDs sample contains pair peaks at wavenumbers of 1354.2 and 1605.1 cm−1, which respectively own to the D and G bonds of the graphitic formation of NCQDs. Peak D and G are associated to the sp3 hybrid bonds of the carbon atoms, which indicates the presence of structural defects in the crystal structure, carbon atoms vibrations with sp2 bonds, respectively. Also, the intensity ratio of D and G peaks (ID/IG) is 0.72. This result shows that the average area size of carbon atoms with sp2 bonds is larger. In other words, the synthesized sample has a graphite structure and fewer structural defects.

Fig. 3 shows the UV–vis and TEM analysis of the synthetic NCQDs samples. According to the absorption diagram, a weak peak at the wavelength of 240 nm and a strong peak at the wavelength of 335 nm have appeared, which are related to the π-π * electron transitions of the sp2 aromatic carbon regions (CC bonds) and n-π * electron transitions of amine/carbonyl functional groups on the surface of NCQDs, respectively. It is worth mentioning that the peak at λ = 335 nm is specific to carbon quantum dot samples doped with nitrogen (Liu et al., 2020). As can be seen from the TEM image in Fig. 3, the NCQDs have a nearly spherical shape with an average diameter of about 14.7 nm.

Fig. 4 shows the results of XRD of the synthesized photocatalysts. At the sample (a) all the observed index peaks at 2θ= 37.33, 43.38, 63.02, 75.60 and 79.61° belonging to BNiO with cubic structure according to the standard card JCPDS NO. = 01–073–1519. Also, in sample (e), the index peaks at 2θ= 28.90, 31.70, 36.70, 45.45, 49.00 and 54.40° belong to Bi7O9I3 phase (Zhang et al., 2018). These results prove the correct synthesis of these two crystalline substances. Fig. 4(b) also shows the XRD pattern of the NCQDs-BNiO sample. As can be seen, the peaks that appear are the same as the BNiO sample (Fig. 4(a)); with the difference that the intensity of the peaks in the composite sample is slightly reduced due to the presence of NCQDs. In the BNiO/Bi7O9I3 sample (Fig. 4(c)), all the peaks of both phases are distinguished. Of course, it is worth mentioning, compared to pure BNiO and Bi7O9I3, in the BNiO/Bi7O9I3 photocatalyst, the intensity of the peaks of both phases has decreased, which is due to the 1:1 wt loading of each phase. It should be noted that in NCQDs-BNiO and NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 samples, the peak related to NCQDs did not appear, which could be due to one of the following reasons: (i) low loading amount of NCQDs (ii) high dispersion of NCQDs nanoparticles (iii) Inherent weakness of the corresponding peak intensity of NCQDs (Jiang et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2015). For better interpretation, the XRD patterns have been magnified in the ranges of 2θ = 27.0–30.5˚ and 2θ = 42.0–44.5˚. In the two samples (c) and (d) as it can be seen, in the range of 2θ= 42.0–44.5˚, the index peak intensity of the BNiO at 2θ= 43.38˚, corresponding to [200] plane, has decreased due to the decrease in the amount of loading and according to the existing magnification in the range of 2θ = 27.0–30.5°, as the weight percentage of Bi7O9I3 decreases, the intensity of its index peak at 2θ = 28.7° decreased. It should be noted that the presence of NCQDs in the structure of the samples (b), (c) and (d) can also lead to a partial decrease in the intensity of the peaks.

Raman spectra of NCQDs-BNiO, NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 and Bi7O9I3 samples can be seen in Fig. 5. The Raman spectrum in Fig. 5(a) is related to NCQDs, as mentioned, it has two peaks corresponding to D and G bonds, respectively. The Raman spectrum of the BNiO photocatalyst (Fig. 5(b)) includes two peaks at wavenumbers 532 and 1022.6 cm−1, the first peak corresponding to the first phonon scattering (1 P, phonon out-of-phase movement in the longitudinal direction) and the second peak corresponds to the second phonon scattering (2 P). Also, according to Fig. 5(d), three peaks were observed in the Raman spectrum of the Bi7O9I3 photocatalyst, Eg stretching modes of the half-bonded Bi-I has shown itself in the peak at the wavenumber of 169.8 cm−1 (Wu et al., 2021). For the NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 sample, according to the spectrum presented in Fig. 5(c), in addition of BNiO and Bi7O9I3 peaks, the peaks related to D and G bonds (in wavenumbers 1396.7 and 1594.9 cm−1 respectively) have appeared which indicates the presence of NCQDs in the structure of the NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3. But compared to the pure sample of NCQDs, these peaks have a red shift, which can be interpreted as the reason for this event: the use of ethylene glycol as a solvent and the sono-solvothermal synthesis method, may reduce a series of oxygenated functional groups which are present in the structure of NCQDs and hence the structure of this material is closer to the graphite state (Gao et al., 2022), which is seen in the process of reducing graphene oxide to reduced graphene oxide. It is also worth mentioning that the ratio of ID and IG bonds (ID/IG) indicates the structural defects and graphitic nature of carbon materials, which in NCQDs and NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 samples are 0.72 and 1.2, respectively; an increase in this ratio indicates an increase in sp3 hybrid orbitals, which has caused an increase in structural defects (Tadesse et al., 2020).

The FTIR spectrum of BNiO, NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 and Bi7O9I3 samples are depicted in Fig. 6 to investigate functional groups of photocatalysts at the range of 1000–4000 cm−1 wave numbers. As can be observed, in all the samples, a broad peak is seen at the wavenumber of 3541 cm−1, which is related to the stretching vibrations of the O-H functional group. The two weak peaks in the wavenumbers of 2854 and 2924 cm−1 are correlated to the stretching and vibrational mode of the C-H functional group bond, which is observed in the pure samples, BNiO and Bi7O9I3; and also, in the nanocomposite due to the presence of a broad peak of NCQDs around 3211 cm−1, it has taken a step-like state and changed its intensity. The two peaks in the 1600.5 and 1630.4 cm−1 for all samples represent the presence of hydroxyl and carbonate functional groups (Liu et al., 2018; Vinoth et al., 2017). The FTIR spectrum of the pure BNiO sample (Fig. 6(a)) has characteristic peaks around the wave numbers of 434 and 565.11 cm−1, which indicates the presence of Ni-O bond. The broad peak at 565.1 cm−1 in the pure Bi7O9I3 sample (Fig. 6(c)) indicates the presence of Bi-O bond, which specify the successful synthesis of this sample, that merges after the formation of heterojunction and shows its presence in the structure of the nanocomposite in the significant and large peak around 495.7 cm−1 (Fig. 6(b)). According to the above results, it can be said that all the peaks in the spectrum of the NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 originate from the pure samples, BNiO, Bi7O9I3 and NCQDs.

FESAM analysis was used to study the surface morphology and structure of BNiO, NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 and Bi7O9I3 samples as shown in Fig. 7. As it is obvious from Fig. 7(a), at 200 nm magnifications, the BNiO nanophotocatalyst particles are in the form of clusters consisting of nanoparticles, and these spherical nanoparticles are more clearly visible at 50 nm magnification. Also, as seen in the magnification of 200 nm in Fig. 7(c), the Bi7O9I3 nanophotocatalyst has a flower-like morphology that consists of nanosheets; these sheets themselves are also made of nanoparticles. Fig. 7(b) is related to the NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 nanophotocatalyst. The coupling of Bi7O9I3 with NCQDs-BNiO, under sono-solvothermal conditions, has caused the uniform distribution and dispersion of NCQDs-BNiO nanoparticles between and on the flower like sheets of Bi7O9I3. This structure and surface morphology facilitates mass transfer and increases the surface area (Observed in BET-BJH analysis) which increases surface adsorption, available active sites and effective adsorption of light and reduces charge carriers recombination (Observed in PL analysis). It is worth mentioning that in the NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 sample, usage of ultrasound waves caused proper dispersion of NCQDs-BNiO nanoparticles.

Fig. 8 shows the three-dimensional analysis of BNiO, NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 and Bi7O9I3 nanophotocatalysts (AFM analysis). The figure related to BNiO confirms the presence of spherical nanoparticles with a size of about 13 nm. The average thickness of plates for NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 and Bi7O9I3 photocatalysts was evaluated about 41 and 32 nm, respectively. The reason for thicker nanosheets in the NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 sample can be attributed to the presence of NCQDs-BNiO particles. Also, as can be seen, as a result of using the ultrasound waves, a proper distribution of particles has been created on the sheets.

For further investigation, the morphology of the selected photocatalyst (NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3), TEM analysis was used, and the results are shown in Fig. 9. According the results morphology of particles can be observed more precisely. These pictures show the presence of NCQDs-BNiO nanoparticles on Bi7O9I3 sheets. In other words, the dark points represent the uniform distribution of NCQDs-BNiO nanoparticles between and on the Bi7O9I3 flower-like photocatalyst sheets, which is caused by usage of ultrasound waves during synthesis. Also, these figures confirm the coupling of NCQDs-BNiO and Bi7O9I3 nanophotocatalysts. The figure which is related to the presence of NCQDs in the structure of selected photocatalyst have been discussed.

BET-BJH analysis for all synthesized nanophotocatalysts based on adsorption and desorption isotherms of nitrogen and pore size distribution diagrams, is presented in Fig. 10. The isotherms of adsorption/desorption for BNiO, Bi7O9I3 and NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 nanophotocatalysts show the conforming of adsorption isotherm type IV with a hysteresis loop according to IUPAC classification, which indicates a structure with meso and macropores. Also, based on the pore size distribution diagram (obtained by BJH method and using the desorption graph of isotherms) the approximate range of pore diameter distribution was determined between 2 and 100 nm, which confirms the presence of meso and macro pores in the structure of nanophotocatalysts with the difference that for the NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 sample, the distribution range of mesopores and their sizes are higher. The presence of these types of pores facilitates the penetration of pollutants and increases the absorption of light with high energy without scattering, which in turn increases the production of electron-hole pairs. According to the hysteresis curve of the isotherms that can be seen in Fig. 10, it’s stated that the hysteresis of the BNiO nanophotocatalyst is the H1 type, which indicates pores in the form of cylindrical channels; In the case of Bi7O9I3 and NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 photocatalysts, the hysteresis is H3 type and that indicates the slit-like pores formed by the sheet like structure of this sample. The specific surface area (SBET) for BNiO, Bi7O9I3 and NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 samples were discerned approximately in the order of 38.7, 29.6 and 44.6 m2/g, respectively. The pore volume (Vtotal) for the hinted samples was determined to about 0.685, 0.177 and 0.209 cm3/g, respectively, and the average pore size (Dp) was determined to about 70.8, 23.9 and 18.8 nm respectively. These holes can facilitate the diffusion of pollutant molecules and products. It is expected that the mass transfer of large molecules of organic pollutant will be facilitated, and due to the high porosity, the surface absorption capacity of pollutant molecules will increase. It is worth mentioning that the large volume of the pores and the meso size of their diameter can cause a larger surface of the nanophotocatalyst to absorb light with an acceptable energy and without reflection. This event will lead to an increase in the production of charge carriers.

Fig. 11 presents the spectrum obtained from DRS analysis for BNiO, B7O9I3 and NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 nanophotocatalysts at the range of 260–710 nm wavelengths. According to the figure, it can be seen that the BNiO nanophotocatalyst will only be active in the ultraviolet spectrum range; While Bi7O9I3 and NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 nanophotocatalysts are active in both ultraviolet and visible spectrum. In other words, Bi7O9I3 and NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 nanophotocatalysts can produce electron-hole pairs by absorbing sunlight. The NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 sample has two optical absorption edges at wavelengths of 373.7 and 526.4 nm, which indicates the formation of a composite between NCQDs-BNiO and Bi7O9I3 phases and the creation of a heterogeneous connection between the phases; Also, compared to other samples, the optical absorption range of the NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 sample is higher. So that the selected nanophotocatalyst (NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3) has the ability to be activated at wavelengths less than 526.4 nm and can produce electron-hole pairs. Enhancement of photon (light) absorption in the selected sample can be assign to the presence of NCQDs in the structure of this nanophotocatalyst. To evaluation the band gap energy of the synthesized nanophotocatalysts, the formula Eg= 1240/λ0 is used, where Eg and λ0 are the band gap energy and the optical adsorption edge wavelength, respectively. In accord with the mentioned equation, the band gap energy of BNiO and Bi7O9I3 samples was 3.47 and 2.42 eV, respectively, and for the NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 nanophotocatalyst, due to the formation of a heterogeneous connection among the pair phases, two band gap energies of 3.32 and 2.36 eV were calculated. As a consequence, because of the formation of a heterogeneous connection between NCQDs-BNiO and Bi7O9I3, BNiO and Bi7O9I3 has red shift at optical absorption edge to higher wavelengths. It is also expected that due to the creation of a suitable heterostructure in the selected composite sample and movement of electrons, the lifetime of charge carriers will increase and the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs will decrease, and as a result, the photocatalytic performance of the mentioned nanophotocatalyst can be improved in the degradation of pharmaceutical pollutants from polluted water under the simulated sunlight.

PL analysis shows the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs excited at a defined wavelength (Asadzadeh-Khaneghah et al., 2018). decreasing in the recombination rate of charge carriers which produced by radiation concluded from the PL spectrum which the intensity should be decreased (Chachvalvutikul et al., 2019; Sene et al., 2020). Fig. 12 shows the PL spectra of BNiO, Bi7O9I3 and NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 nanophotocatalysts. As could be seen, the spectrum of the NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 sample indicates enhancement in separation and transfer efficiency of charge carriers and reduce in their recombination rate which can be assigned to the effective coupling of phases by the sono-solvothermal method which is decorated by NCQDs that has a special effect on surface reactions and improves the performance of the photocatalyst.

Before the photocatalytic degradation, the surface adsorption of BNiO, NCQDs-BNiO, BNiO/Bi7O9I3, NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 and Bi7O9I3 nanophotocatalysts was evaluated after 210 min in a dark place. According to the results presented in Fig. 13 (a), the amount of surface adsorption for the synthesized samples was about 83.9%, 77.7%, 58.1%, 53.5% and 70.0%, respectively. The highest adsorption rate belongs to the BNiO sample, which can be assigned to the high specific surface area and larger volume pores (especially macro pores), which is confirmed by the results of BET-BJH analysis. The photocatalytic degradation performance of the synthesized samples (BNiO, NCQDs-BNiO, BNiO/Bi7O9I3, NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 and Bi7O9I3) were assessed to eliminate tetracycline hydrochloride antibiotic pollutant from contaminated aqueous solution presented in Fig. 13 (b). As observed, the percentage of degradation obtained by the synthesized samples for 100 mL of tetracycline hydrochloride solution was 37.9%, 53.8%, 66.2%, 73.9% and 81.6% respectively. The increased performance of the degradation process by the NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 sample can be attributed to the finer particles and proper dispersion of NCQDs-BNiO, favourable surface absorption, the effective formation of indirect heterogeneous connection of the z-scheme between the phases during irradiation, the presence of NCQDs as the electron donor/acceptor (reducing the electron-hole pair recombination) and the appropriate band gap energy (according to DRS analysis). This effective connection increases the absorption of light, accelerates the separation of charge carriers, increases their lifespan and reduces their recombination. Also, the usage of ultrasound waves did a proper dispensation of NCQDs-BNiO photocatalyst nanoparticles on Bi7O9I3 nanoplates, which cause the enhancement of accessible sites; and the facilitation of diffusion phenomenon, increasing pollutant surface adsorption and effective light harvesting with incoming energy done by presence of meso pores, which cause the enhancement at the production of electron-hole pairs; so the photodegradation of tetracycline hydrochloride was boosted for selected sample because of efficacious happing of surface reactions, facilitation of mass transfer and surface adsorption, which shows the key effect of the created structural model and the usage of ultrasound waves on the physical and chemical properties of synthetic nanophotocatalyst and its performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity in elimination of antibiotics
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of the photocatalytic degradation of other antibiotics, such as ofloxacin, levofloxacin, and ciprofloxacin, was evaluated to further investigate the photocatalytic ability of NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 nanocomposite. The characteristics of these antibiotics are presented in Table 1. According to the results presented in Fig. 14 (a), the surface adsorption/desorption equilibrium for pharmaceutical pollutants: tetracycline hydrochloride, ofloxacin, levofloxacin, and ciprofloxacin by NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 nanophotocatalyst after 210 min was about 53.5%, 71.8%, 80.80% and 81.7%, respectively. Also, conforming to Fig. 14 (b), the degradation efficiency of the mentioned pollutants after 180 min of light irradiation was calculated to be about 81.6%, 73.6%, 73.4% and 71.5%, respectively. This difference can be attributed to the type/number of hetero atoms, the spatial structure of the molecules and how the atoms are bonded. In Fig. 15, the absorbance spectrum changes in UV–vis region related to the tetracycline hydrochloride during the degradation process using NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 nanophotocatalyst are depicted. As observed, the absorption peak intensity decreased with increasing reaction time and finally, the intensity of the absorption peak at the wavelength of 359 nm (characteristic peak) almost disappeared after a period of 180 min and became smooth. In addition, no new absorption peak was observed in the UV–vis region, indicating the removal of the pharmaceutical contaminant without producing any side products.

Table 2 shows the comparison of the present work and the studies done in the direction of photocatalytic removal of antibiotics and fluoroquinolones. As mentioned earlier simulated sunlight, cheaper precursors and also a relatively smaller amount of photocatalyst has been used for the photocatalytic removal of various pollutants such as tetracycline hydrochloride (70 mg/L), ofloxacin (20 mg/L), levofloxacin (20 mg/L) and ciprofloxacin (20 mg/L). So NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 sample has shown an acceptable performance compared to other photocatalysts.

The effect of NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 nanophotocatalyst loading, on the degradation efficiency of tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) antibiotic pollutant from contaminated aqueous solution is reported in Fig. 16. The degradation of TCH with an initial concentration of 70 mg/L with environmental pH conditions for loadings of 0.5 and 1 g/L was obtained to be 70.6% and 81.6%, respectively. The obtained results can be analysed as follows: by increasing the amount of loading from 0.5 to 1 g/L, the number of active sites available for pollutant adsorption and degradation will increase and thus the degradation percentage will also increase. The effect of the initial concentration of the antibiotic pollutant on the degradation efficiency of tetracycline hydrochloride using NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 nanophotocatalyst is presented in Fig. 16. In order to carry out this investigation, the initial concentrations of 70 and 100 mg/L of tetracycline hydrochloride antibiotic pollutant were selected, which in the same conditions (catalyst loading 1 g/L and at environmental pH) they have reached degradation percentages of 81.6% and 74.3%, respectively. The results show that the efficiency of degradation decreases with the increase of pollutant concentration which can be stated with the increase in pollutant concentration, the amount of pollutant adsorbed on the surface of the photocatalyst increases. This causes the reduction of active species, such as oxygen radicals, which are created by the photocatalyst surface activity which exposed to radiation of light. On the other hand, with the surging of contaminant concentration, the number of photons adsorbed by the contaminant will rise. Therefore, fewer photons reach the surface of the photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studying CO2 conversion to CH3OH over NCQDs-black NiO/Bi7O9I3
ce:section.ce:para: Thermodynamically, according to reaction CO2 + 2 H2O → CH3OH + O2, the energy required to convert one mole of carbon dioxide into MeOH is equal to 288 kj (Mohamed et al., 2021). In Fig. 17, the production rate of MeOH has been shown with the passage of time for mesopores nanosheet Z-Scheme NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 sample. In this study photoreduction yield of selected sample was analyzed which could produce 43.437 µmol/gcatalyst methanol over the 8 h. Other products, such as formaldehyde and formic acid, were not identified due to the low amount of production.

Photocatalyst stability is a determining factor for its evaluation and practical application. The stability and reusability of the photocatalyst was evaluated by the photodegradation test of tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) from the synthetic aqueous solution, under simulated sunlight, during four cycles. The results are reported as percentage of activity in Fig. 18. After each cycle, the photocatalyst was separated from the remaining solution by a centrifuge, washed with deionized water and dried at room temperature, and then the used photocatalyst is used in the next cycle. The activity rate was set on using the following equation:
(2)
Activity % =
Remediation
i
Remediation
0
× 100

In Eq. (2), Remediation(i) and Remediation(0) are photocatalytic degradation efficiency (adsorption + degradation) in each cycle and photocatalytic degradation efficiency of the first cycle, respectively.

As can be observed ( Fig. 19), the performance of photocatalyst decline due to the following reasons: leaching phenomenon, poisoning of the photocatalyst with pollutant and production of by-products by 15.6% after the last cycle.

Finally, at the end of the fourth cycle, the XRD analysis of the used photocatalyst was compared with the XRD analysis of the fresh photocatalyst, as it is clear from Fig. 19 that the structure of the used photocatalyst has not changed significantly compared to the fresh photocatalyst; The only difference is the decrease in the intensity of some of the index peaks in the used sample, which can be attributed to the leaching phenomenon.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible reaction mechanism over NCQDs-black NiO/Bi7O9I3
ce:section.ce:para: In order to describe the reaction mechanism and decomposition of the pollutants, reactor tests were conducted to determine the species participating in the degradation process. The types that are often considered are: holes, electrons, hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide radical (•O2 -); Therefore, ammonium oxalate (AO) was used as the inhibitor of holes, benzoquinone (BQ) as an •O2 - inhibitor and isopropyl alcohol as an •OH inhibitor (Haghighi et al., 2021; Kohansal et al., 2023; Shabani et al., 2019). As can be seen in Fig. 20, with the addition of AO, the efficiency of the system decreased from 81.6% to 35.2% after 180 min, which shows the key role of holes in the degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) by NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 nanophotocatalyst. Also, with the addition of BQ and isopropyl alcohol, the degradation efficiency decreased from 81.6% to 65.9% and 81.6–76.2%, respectively which indicates the role of superoxide and hydroxyl radicals in the pollutant degradation process.

According to the mentioned tests and analyses presented in the previous sections, a glance at the Fig. 21 illustrates the removal mechanism of tetracycline hydrochloride by NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 nanophotocatalyst under simulated solar light. By applying Eqs. (3) and (4) (Ma et al., 2021; Sanni et al., 2021), the potential energies of the valence and conduction band of BNiO and Bi7O9I3 photocatalysts (with the bandgap energy of 3.47 and 2.42 eV, respectively) were calculated.

In the above equations, X, EVB, ECB, Ee and Eg are the absolute electronegativity of the semiconductor, the potential energy of the valence band, the potential energy of the conduction band, the free electron energy based on hydrogen (Ee=4.5 eV) and the band gap energy, respectively. The X value was calculated using the electronegativity of each element in the photocatalyst.

According to the above equations, ECB for two nanophotocatalysts BNiO and Bi7O9I3 were calculated as − 0.48 and + 0.37 eV, respectively, and EVB was calculated as + 2.99 and + 2.79 eV, subsequently. Experience has shown that the situation of the photocatalyst band gaps energy and the formation of heterogeneous bonds cause the separation of charge carriers. When the photocatalyst is exposed to sunlight and receives energy equal to or greater than the band gap energy, the electrons in the valence band of both semiconductors are transferred to their conduction band and instead of them; holes remain in the valence band. Although the energy related to the minimum conduction layer of BNiO is more negative than Bi7O9I3; but since the minimum wavelength emitted from the light origin is 315 nm, coequal to 3.9 eV; so cause the arousal and transmission of BNiO and Bi7O9I3 valence bond electrons to the maximum energy (−0.93 and −1.14 eV, respectively). The electrons in the BNiO conduction bond can move towards the NCQDs nanoparticles (as electron donors and acceptors) and so the separation efficiency of the generated charge carriers increases and their recombination speed decreases. The electrons on the NCQDs surface may also be consumed by the holes of the Bi7O9I3 valence bond, hence, the conduction layer electrons of BNiO have a greater ability to create oxygen radicals (O2/•O2 - = −0.33 eV) than the conduction layer of Bi7O9I3. Also, electrons that are placed at energy stages lower than + 0.7 eV can convert O2 molecules into hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). On the other hand, as can be seen in Fig. 21, according to the standard potential of the half-reactions -OH/•OH (+1.99 eV) and H2O/•OH (+2.27 eV), the holes in the valence bond of BNiO can attack water molecules and hydroxyl ions and produce hydroxyl radicals (•OH). In this way, with the formation of an indirect z-scheme under the influence of radiation, finally, radicals (hydroxyl and superoxide), holes and hydrogen peroxide can react with pollutants which adsorbed on the surface of the photocatalyst and cause the destruction of pollutants. According to the explanations above, the following reactions can be proposed to degradation of antibiotic pollutants:

Besides, as shown in Fig. 21, the electrons existing in the conduction band of Bi7O9I3 can produce the CH3OH (CO2/CH3OH = −0.38 eV). It is worth noting that the necessary H+ could provide by the oxidation of H2O molecules to O2 on the valance band of BNiO.

In order to investigate the kinetic of the photocatalytic degradation reaction of tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) by the synthesized nanophotocatalysts, the pseudo-first order equation was used (Eq.14) (Gao et al., 2023; Pirsaheb et al., 2022; Sadeghpour et al., 2022). The kapp (min−1) is apparent rate constant and t is irradiation time (min).

As can be seen in Fig. 22, the constant apparent reaction rate for BNiO, NCQDs-BNiO, Bi7O9I3, BNiO/Bi7O9I3, NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 samples were calculated 0.005, 0.0063, 0.011, 0.0083 and 0.0137 min−1, respectively. Among the synthesized samples, the NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 sample has the highest constant apparent reaction rate, which is confirmed by the performed analyzes.

A new black nickel oxide nanophotocatalyst decorated with nitrogen-carbon quantum dot and coupled with Bi7O9I3 was prepared by facile sono-solvothermal method and its photo-degradation performance under the simulated sunlight irradiation for the degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride and other fluoroquinolones was investigated. Beside it the photoreduction of selected photocatalyst was evaluated. Decorating BNiO with NCQDs and creating indirect Z-scheme led to unique optical, structural and physicochemical properties such as proper morphology with structural defects, red shift of the light absorption region, creating a special type of electron recombination and changing the structure of pores. The NCQDs-BNiO/Bi7O9I3 sample with highest surface area (44.6 m2/g) and mesopores distribution showed more acceptable activity compared to other synthesized samples so that it could remove tetracycline hydrochloride, ofloxacin, levofloxacin and ciprofloxacin from synthetic by degradation efficiency of 81.6%, 73.6%, 73.4% and 71.5%, respectively and also could produce 43.437 µmol/gcatalyst of MeOH.
